beats.prompt: Back in Watch the First Heads you found that a fair coin takes 2 flips on average until the first Heads. Match each statement to its value.

feedback.correct: Exactly — E[H] = 2 was an expected value all along. Now we name the move.
hints: Tap a phrase, then its matching value.
The geometric wait is 1/p; with p = 1/2 that gives E = 2.
Match: 'Average flips until first H' → E[H] = 2; 'P(H) on any flip' → 1/2.

beats.prompt: A casino offers you one roll of a fair die, paid that many dollars. A ticket costs $3. Before computing: is a $3 ticket a good deal, a bad deal, or exactly fair?

A good deal.note: Good instinct — let's prove it: compute the average payoff and check it beats the $3 ticket.

A bad deal.note: Let's test it — if the average payoff tops $3, the ticket's a bargain. Let's compute it.

Exactly fair.note: Let's test it — '$3 is the middle of 1–6' skips the weighting; the balance point sits a half-dollar higher.

beats.prompt: Quick reminder before we calculate — read the card, then continue.

interaction.title: Weighted averages
interaction.body: A plain average treats every value equally — that is a special case where each weight is 1/n. A weighted average scales each value by how likely it is: multiply each value by its weight, then sum. When the weights are probabilities, you get the expected value.

feedback.correct: Weighted average: each value scaled by its probability.
hints: Read the card.
Weight = probability = how often that value occurs.
Continue when ready.

beats.prompt: One roll of a fair die — you are paid the face value. What is the expected payoff? Enter as a fraction (e.g. 3/2).

feedback.correct: E = (1+2+3+4+5+6)/6 = 21/6 = 7/2 = $3.50 — a half-dollar above the $3 ticket. The ticket is a steal.
hints: The average needn't be rollable. Sum 1+…+6 = 21, divide by 6 → 3.5.

beats.prompt: Tap each face value to place its probability weight on the beam. Watch the fulcrum slide to the balance point.

hero.structuralReadout: E[X] = 7/2 — the fair die's balance point.

feedback.correct: The die balances at E[X] = 7/2 — that is the fair price, not the visual midpoint of the labels.
hints: Tap each number to place its weight (1/6 for a fair die).
Each of the six faces is equally likely: P(x) = 1/6.
Place all six weights and watch the beam settle.

beats.interviewNote: Interview framing: E[X|X>0] = √(2/π) ≈ 0.798 for X∼N(0,1) shows EV can be irrational; the clean rationals here (7/2, 7) are a feature of these toy distributions, not a general rule. The 'fair price' of any bet equals its expected value — casinos profit on the delta between the ticket price and E[X].
beats.prompt: You just saw it on the beam. Now name it.

interaction.title: E[X] = Σ x · P(x)
interaction.body:
The expected value of a discrete random variable X is the probability-weighted sum of all its outcomes — list each possible value x, multiply by its probability P(x), and add.

For a continuous variable (e.g. X∼N(0,1) truncated to X > 0), the sum becomes an integral: E[X] = ∫ x f(x) dx. For that distribution, E[X|X>0] = √(2/π) ≈ 0.798 — irrational. The clean fractions 7/2 and 7 in this lesson are a feature of these particular toy distributions, not a general rule.

feedback.correct: E[X] = Σ x·P(x) — the balance point of the distribution, not the midpoint of its labels.
hints: Read the formula card.
The balance point of the distribution, not the midpoint of the label range.
Tap the continuous-analogue disclosure to see why EV can be irrational.

beats.prompt: Watch many die rolls. Does the running average settle where the balance beam predicted?

feedback.correct: The empirical mean converges to E[X] = 3.5 — the balance point is the long-run average. This is the Law of Large Numbers.
hints: Watch the running mean (jagged line) drift toward the theory line at 3.5.
Run more trials — the convergence tightens.
Long-run frequency = theoretical expected value.

beats.prompt: Roll two fair dice. (1) How many ordered pairs (d₁, d₂) sum to 5? Express as P(sum = 5). (2) A separate game pays $3 with probability 1/4, $5 with probability 1/2, $7 with probability 1/4. What is E[payout]?

feedback.correct: P(sum=5)=4/36=1/9 — four ordered pairs (1+4, 2+3, 3+2, 4+1). E[payout]=3·(1/4)+5·(1/2)+7·(1/4)=20/4=5. The 11 two-dice sums are NOT equally likely — their counts differ.
hints: The eleven sums aren't equally likely. Count the ways each sum happens, then weight by those counts.
For sum=5: (1,4),(2,3),(3,2),(4,1) — that is 4 ordered pairs out of 36. For E: multiply each payout by its probability and add.

beats.interviewNote: Source: classic dice-sum pmf drill (Blitzstein & Hwang, Introduction to Probability, §4 — sum of two fair dice generalized to s sides). Symmetry shortcut: the {2…8} pmf is symmetric about 5, so E pins to the mirror point 5; the long Σx·P(x) certifies it.
beats.prompt: Mastery challenge (fresh): two fair FOUR-sided dice (d4). The seven possible sums 2 through 8 carry triangular weights (1, 2, 3, 4, 3, 2, 1)/16. Compute E[X] = Σ x·P(x). Enter a whole number or simplified fraction.

interaction.scenario: Two fair d4 — sum the full non-uniform pmf. Weights peak at \(P(5)=\tfrac{4}{16}\). The midpoint of 2–8 is 5 by symmetry. Is \(E[X]\) always the midpoint? Compute the weighted sum to find out.

feedback.correct: The general formula sums every outcome times its probability: \[ E[X] = \sum_{x=2}^{8} x \cdot P(x) \] With triangular weights \( P(k) = \tfrac{4-|k-5|}{16} \), the numerator is \( 2\cdot1+3\cdot2+4\cdot3+5\cdot4+6\cdot3+7\cdot2+8\cdot1 = 80 \), so \( E[X] = \tfrac{80}{16} = 5 \). The pmf is symmetric around 5 — symmetry always pins the mean to the mirror point, no matter how many sides the dice have.
hints: Don't eyeball the middle face. Sum \(x \cdot P(x)\) over the triangular pmf of the two-d4 sum.
Group by symmetry: \(P(k)=P(10-k)\). Each mirror pair \((k,\,10-k)\) contributes \(10 \cdot P(k)\); the peak \(P(5)=\tfrac{4}{16}\) stands alone, pinning the mean to 5.
\( \tfrac{2\cdot1+3\cdot2+4\cdot3+5\cdot4+6\cdot3+7\cdot2+8\cdot1}{16} = \tfrac{80}{16} = 5 \).

beats.interviewNote: Shortcut check: by linearity of expectation (next lesson), E[sum of two dice] = E[die₁]+E[die₂] = 7/2+7/2 = 7. The triangular-pmf Σ x·P(x) certifies the same answer the hard way — these two routes agreeing is the sanity check.
beats.prompt: Mastery challenge — the sum of two fair dice, the hard way. The table shows all 11 outcomes (sums 2 through 12) with their triangular weights (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1)/36. Compute E[X] = Σ x·P(x). Enter a whole number or simplified fraction.

interaction.scenario: Two fair dice — sum the full non-uniform pmf. Weights peak at \(P(7)=\tfrac{6}{36}\). The midpoint of 2–12 is 7 by symmetry. Is \(E[X]\) always the midpoint? Compute to find out.

feedback.correct: The general formula sums every outcome times its probability: \[ E[X] = \sum_{x=2}^{12} x \cdot P(x) \] With triangular weights \( P(k) = \tfrac{6-|k-7|}{36} \), the numerator is \( 2\cdot1+3\cdot2+4\cdot3+5\cdot4+6\cdot5+7\cdot6+8\cdot5+9\cdot4+10\cdot3+11\cdot2+12\cdot1 = 252 \), so \( E[X] = \tfrac{252}{36} = 7 \). The pmf is symmetric around 7 — symmetry always pins the mean to the mirror point.
hints: Eyeballing the middle gives 6, but the weights peak at 7. Sum \(x \cdot P(x)\) over the triangular pmf.
Group by symmetry: \(P(k)=P(14-k)\). Each pair \((k,\,14-k)\) contributes \(14 \cdot P(k)\); then \(P(7)=\tfrac{6}{36}\) stands alone.
\( \tfrac{2\cdot1+3\cdot2+4\cdot3+5\cdot4+6\cdot5+7\cdot6+8\cdot5+9\cdot4+10\cdot3+11\cdot2+12\cdot1}{36} = \tfrac{252}{36} = 7 \).

beats.prompt: The move that ran through this whole lesson.

feedback.correct: List → weight → add: E[X]=Σ x·P(x). The balance point of the distribution, not the midpoint of its labels. Next: how expectations add — even when things are tangled.
hints: Read each recap card.
The three-step move (list → weight → add) runs every expected-value question.
Tap to continue.
